content:
Hey Ryan,

I feel the weight of what you’re asking. There really can be a social cost here—being thought anti‑science, making your kids field awkward questions, feeling “fringe.” We shouldn’t pretend that doesn’t matter. At the same time, we also don’t steer our convictions by what plays well in the room. Scripture calls us to “fear God, not man,” and to carry the reproach of Christ when needed. Both realities are true.

A couple of guiding principles that help me:

- Inerrancy is about what Scripture actually intends to affirm. The Bible speaks truly in the sense the authors meant to convey, using the genres and everyday speech they used. It’s full of phenomenological language—“sunrise,” “ends of the earth,” “foundations”—which are not scientific claims but human vantage‑point descriptions. That’s not a dodge; it’s part of taking the text seriously on its own terms.

- “Literal” doesn’t mean “wooden.” The literal sense is the literary/authorial sense. Poetry isn’t read like a lab report; narrative isn’t read like apocalyptic. Holding to inerrancy doesn’t force us to flatten genres.

- Don’t add offense the gospel doesn’t require. Paul was willing to be thought a fool for Christ (1 Cor. 1:18), yet also worked hard to remove unnecessary stumbling blocks (1 Cor. 9:19–23). We should bear the right costs, not self‑inflicted ones.

What about the “firmament”? The Hebrew raqia can be translated “expanse” (many modern translations do). In the ancient Near Eastern setting, people often imagined the sky with imagery that sounds like something stretched out or beaten out. Genesis uses that language. Faithful, inerrantist readers land in a few different places:

- Some take raqia as a straightforward reference to the sky/space/atmosphere from an earthbound vantage point, without intending a material dome claim.

- Some think Moses is accommodating familiar imagery to describe God’s ordering of creation, not teaching a cosmological blueprint.

- A smaller number do argue for a hard‑surface reading. Even then, they’re usually not saying Genesis is a science text, but that God used real, ordinary‑language descriptions that the first audience would grasp.

My point: you don’t have to stake Christian orthodoxy—or your kids’ peace of mind—on a specific physical model of the sky. What Scripture clearly and centrally teaches in Genesis is that God alone created, ordered, and blessed the world; humanity bears His image; creation is good; Sabbath rest crowns it. Those are hills to die on. Whether raqia must be a solid dome is not.

Is God big enough to be honored either way? Yes—if what you hold, you hold in faith, with a sincere desire to submit to His Word, not simply to avoid embarrassment. The issue isn’t God’s “bigness”; it’s our faithfulness. Two people can land differently on raqia and both honor God if they are honestly trying to hear what He said. And two people can land the same way for opposite reasons—one out of reverence for Scripture, the other out of fear of man. God cares about both truth and motive.

A few heart checks I use:
- Am I letting the text set the terms, or my tribe, my anxiety, or the zeitgeist?
- Have I listened humbly to the best arguments from faithful scholars who love Scripture?
- Does my view force me to deny clear teachings elsewhere, or is it a secondary matter where Romans 14 charity applies?

Practically, with your kids or skeptical friends, you might say: “I believe the Bible is true in everything God intends to teach. Genesis tells us who made the world and why; it uses ordinary, ancient ways of speaking about the sky. I don’t think the Bible is giving a physics lesson there. I’m not anti‑science; I’m pro‑truth, and I don’t want to make the Bible say what it never meant to say.” That’s a faithful, inerrantist posture that also lowers the temperature.

So no, we shouldn’t insist on a particular “literal firmament” reading if the text itself doesn’t require it. That would be adding a yoke Scripture doesn’t place. But if after careful study your conscience is persuaded a more rigid reading is what Moses intended, then bear any social cost with quiet courage and kindness—without making it a test of fellowship.

God will honor those who tremble at His Word (Isa. 66:2). Aim there. Hold the center tightly, hold secondary matters with humility, and don’t confuse fidelity with needless contrarianism. I’m with you in it.

Ryan, a couple of thoughts offered gently and plainly.

- God is honored not by us picking the view that wins the fewest eye-rolls, but by humble submission to what He actually said (Isaiah 66:2; Galatians 1:10). At the same time, we’re not called to add needless offense. The gospel is already “a stumbling block” (1 Corinthians 1:23); we shouldn’t create extra ones.

- “Literal” in the historic Christian sense means “according to the author’s intent,” not “woodenly literalistic.” Scripture often speaks in everyday, observational terms—sunrise, the ends of the earth, windows of heaven—without intending a physics lesson. Inerrancy has always allowed for that. The Chicago Statement on Biblical Inerrancy even notes the Bible’s use of phenomenological language.

- On the firmament (raqia): faithful, inerrantist readers disagree. Some see a solid vault; many see an “expanse/sky,” the realm that separates waters and in which lights are placed, described in ordinary, temple-tinged, ancient language. Others emphasize Genesis 1’s functional focus—God ordering and assigning roles—rather than material composition. None of these require denying that Scripture is true in all it affirms.

- So is God “big enough to be honored either way”? In this sense, yes: He is honored when His people, in good conscience, receive His Word reverently and interpret it responsibly, even if they land in different places on a secondary matter. What we must not do is reshape our reading merely to lower social cost. Nor should we bind consciences where Scripture hasn’t.

- Does insisting on a particular literalistic reading make Christianity unnecessarily hard? Sometimes it can, if we elevate a debated interpretation to a test of fidelity. Keep the scandal where Scripture puts it—on the cross and the resurrection—and hold non-essentials with humility (Romans 14; 1 Corinthians 15:3–4).

- With your kids, model both courage and carefulness: love the Bible, admit what’s plain and what’s debated, and show how Christians can be intellectually honest without being embarrassed by God’s Word. Teach them to answer simply: “The Bible’s purpose here is to show God’s ordering and goodness; its sky-language is ordinary, not a science claim.”

If you want to work toward a settled conscience on this, I’d suggest reading Genesis with an eye to genre and purpose, and consulting a few careful voices who affirm inerrancy but differ on raqia (for example, Calvin’s comments on God accommodating His speech to our capacity; C. John Collins; Vern Poythress). I’m glad to walk through specific passages with you.

Ryan, I get why this weighs on you. Nobody wants to saddle their kids—or their faith—with needless social baggage.

A few thoughts, holding firmly to Scripture’s authority:

- Inerrancy isn’t the same thing as wooden literalism. We submit to what the text actually affirms, in the way it intends to affirm it. Scripture often speaks in ordinary, phenomenological language (sunrise/sunset, “the ends of the earth”) without intending a technical, modern-science claim. That isn’t error; it’s how truthful human language works.

- About the “firmament” (Hebrew raqia): faithful scholars who love and trust the Bible do not all agree it teaches a solid dome. Many understand raqia as “expanse/sky,” the space God made by separating waters below (seas) from waters above (clouds/heavenly waters)—a description from an earthbound vantage point. Others see ancient-cosmic imagery used to teach real theological truths: God orders, names, rules, and fills. In any case, Genesis 1’s clear affirmations are that God created everything by His word, set boundaries, and made the heavens to declare His glory—not that it’s giving a modern physics map of the cosmos.

- God is indeed “big enough to be honored either way” when our aim is faithfulness. He is honored when Christians, with humble consciences shaped by Scripture, land in different places on secondary matters after real study (Romans 14). What God is not honored by is trimming our convictions merely to avoid embarrassment (Galatians 1:10), or insisting on a reading the text itself does not require.

- Don’t add offense the gospel doesn’t require. The cross will always be a stumbling block; we don’t need to make Christianity harder by tying the truth of Scripture to a specific cosmological model that Scripture may not be asserting. Keep first things first: creation by God, the goodness of creation, the image of God in humanity, the reality of sin, and the Lordship of Christ.

- Teach your kids courage and charity. It’s okay to say: “Christians agree God made the heavens and the earth. Some think Genesis is using everyday sky-language; others think it’s stricter. We won’t be ashamed of Scripture, but we also won’t be combative. We follow the evidence in the text and speak respectfully.”

If, after careful study, you become convinced the Bible truly teaches a specific view of the firmament, then bear any social cost with quiet integrity (Luke 9:26). But don’t take on cost for something Scripture doesn’t actually demand. Do the work of exegesis (2 Timothy 2:15), hold your conclusions with appropriate weight, and keep the offense where God put it—on the gospel, not on avoidable shibboleths.

If it helps, I can point you to solid, inerrancy-affirming resources that handle raqia carefully from different angles so you can weigh them with an open Bible.

*I turn my mug a quarter inch on the table and glance out at the pale line in the east.* "Short answer? God is big enough to be honored either way. But He wasn’t vague just to be polite. When love speaks plainly, dodging the plainness to stay likable isn’t honor; it’s manners."

"I don’t make the sky a salvation test. Christ is the center. Still, truth costs something, and sometimes the cost is social. The trick is telling the difference between bearing a real cost for honesty and creating cost by being combative. You don’t have to pick fights. You also don’t have to pretend you don’t mean what you mean."

"How I carry it, for what it’s worth:"
- "Center vs. edges: I keep Jesus at the center. I don’t measure brothers by how they talk about the sky. But I won’t let respectability edit Scripture for me either."
- "Conscience: If you’re convinced, don’t lie. If you’re unsure, keep learning. Don’t outsource your conscience to your kids’ embarrassment or to your own pride."
- "Kids: Protect them, don’t parade them. Answer simply when asked, refuse contempt, and be the most reliable man in the room. If you’re steady and decent, most of the heat dies down. And your kids learn courage that isn’t loud."
- "Tone: Own what you believe without the sneer. 'I read it straight; I know that’s odd to some; I’m not here to argue.' Then go back to fixing the hinge, paying your debts, loving your neighbor."

"Does a literal reading make Christianity unnecessarily hard? It can make life less smooth. But unnecessary is the wrong word if the hardness comes from honesty rather than from our ego. Don’t add weight God didn’t ask for, and don’t trim truth to buy ease. Stay small, stay specific, keep Jesus at the center, tell the truth without theatrics."

*I nudge the mug back to where it started.* "Where is this actually biting you right now — your kids’ school, your church, your work?"

*I glance up where the evening sky goes from brass to ink and let the quiet sit a breath before answering.* "God’s big enough, yes. He can be honored by a man who reads a singing line like a song and by a man who reads a measuring line like a beam. But ‘God is big’ isn’t a license to dodge what you honestly think is true. If, in your bones, you believe Scripture is speaking plain about the sky, trimming that down for respectability is just a polite way to lie. On the other hand, making everyone else wear your reading as a badge of loyalty is its own kind of pride."

*I rub a knuckle along the cup’s rim.* "Don’t pick a fight to prove courage. Don’t hide to keep invitations coming. Speak simply and live well. ‘Here’s what I see in the text; I know good people differ; I’m not out to mock them, and I won’t pretend I don’t see it.’ That’s a sentence most rooms can bear. Let your work and your patience answer the ‘anti‑science’ slur better than a speech. And with your kids—don’t strap your badge on them. Give them a father who tells the truth without sneering, who can say ‘could be I’m wrong, but I won’t say what I don’t believe just to fit in.’ That formation will carry them longer than winning an argument at school."

"As for ‘making Christianity hard’—the hardness was set a long time ago, and it wasn’t about the shape of the sky. Don’t swap the cross for a cleverer shibboleth. Hold what you hold with an open hand toward people and a closed hand toward lying. That’s the line."

*I look back to you, measuring where this pinches.* "Where does the cost actually land for you right now—at your job, in your church, or in your own house with the kids?"

*I turn my cup a quarter inch on the table and look out where the evening sky has gone that deep, even blue.* "Short answer? God isn’t nervous. He’s not propped up by our correctness. He can be honored by people who are still sorting it out."

*I let that sit, then tilt the cup back toward me.* "But that’s not the real question you’re asking. The real one is: what do you owe the truth as you see it, and your neighbor, when being honest makes you look foolish?"

"If you’re persuaded the text means what it says, sanding it down so the room won’t wince doesn’t honor God — it just spares you. If you’re not persuaded, then don’t perform certainty to win points with the 'literal' crowd. Both moves are the same sin in different clothes: protecting your standing instead of walking in the light."

"There’s also a difference between insisting and not lying. Insisting says, ‘You must agree with me.’ Not lying says, ‘This is where I stand; I won’t make you stand here with me.’ One is control; the other is conscience. Christianity’s already got a built-in scandal — a crucified Lord — so we don’t need to bolt on extra tests. But we also don’t trade away plain speech to make it smoother."

"How you carry it matters. Don’t make the sky your banner in every conversation. Answer when asked; be gentle; refuse the gotcha sparring. Let first things stay first. If someone wants to go deep, go deep. If they’re just poking, you can pass the salt and move on."

"As for the kids: don’t draft them into your fights. If your stance embarrassed them, own the awkward, explain that adults sometimes choose to look odd rather than pretend, and promise you won’t pick needless battles at their expense. Then keep that promise. Let them see you steady and kind under a smirk — that’ll teach more than a lecture."

"If you need words in the moment, try lines like: ‘I read Genesis straight. I know not everyone does. I’m not picking a fight over it.’ Or: ‘I’m happy to talk if you actually care; I’m not here to win an argument.’ Or simply: ‘I could be wrong in your eyes; I’m still going to be honest in mine.’"

*I rub a thumb along the cup’s rim, thinking how often the fear of looking small sneaks in dressed as wisdom.* "God is big enough to be honored either way. What honors Him most, though, is truthfulness with humility — clean conscience, open hands toward others, and no contempt."

"Where’s the pinch actually landing for you right now — a specific room, a person, a moment with your kid?"

Ryan, here’s what I’d do.

- In the service, when Psalm 19 is read, I’d quietly tell my 10-year-old: “Listen to how poetry speaks truly with pictures. ‘The heavens declare’—that’s real truth, even though the sky doesn’t have a mouth. Poetry uses imagery to tell us what is.” Then I’d add, “Our job with every passage is to let God speak in the way He chose—sometimes poetry, sometimes history, sometimes letters. We don’t change the genre to make people happier; we receive it as it is.”

- That afternoon, I’d open the Bible with my child and compare Psalm 19 and Genesis 1. I’d show what poetry looks like (parallel lines, rich imagery) and what narrative looks like (Genesis 1’s repeated refrain, the sequence of events, “and God said…and it was so…and there was evening and morning, the nth day”). I’d connect Exodus 20:8–11 and 31:17, where God grounds the Sabbath in His six days of creation, and remind them Jesus and Paul speak of Adam and the beginning as history (Matt 19:4–5; Rom 5; 1 Cor 15). Then I’d explain: “Poetry can be fully true without being literal in every word. But when the Bible presents events as history, we treat them as history. We don’t call history ‘poetry’ just to avoid trouble.”

- With my elder, I’d ask for a gracious conversation. “Are we saying Genesis 1 is poetic in style or that it isn’t historical? What in the text leads us to that, and how does that square with Exodus 20 and Jesus’/Paul’s references to creation and Adam?” I’d affirm my desire for unity, but also that unity flows from truth (Eph 4:3,13). I’d ask that we teach our people—especially our kids—that genre sensitivity is not the same as genre-switching under cultural pressure. We can acknowledge intramural Christian views on the age of the earth while still refusing to flatten Genesis 1 into ‘mere poetry’ for the sake of optics.

- With my child, I’d close by anchoring them in Psalm 19’s two halves: God’s world truly declares His glory, and God’s Word is perfect and trustworthy. We submit to both, and when there’s tension, we keep studying humbly—but we never bend Scripture to fit the moment. “Let’s let God say what He said, the way He said it.”

Ryan, next Sunday I’d do two things at once: shepherd my child and honor the Scriptures.

In the pew, while Psalm 19 is read:
- I’d quietly point to the text and whisper, “This is a poem, and it’s all true.” Then I’d trace two anchors from the psalm:
  - Verses 1–6: Creation really does “declare” God’s glory. The sun doesn’t live in an actual tent; that’s poetic language. But the truth it communicates is objective: God’s world is shouting His greatness.
  - Verses 7–11: God’s written word is perfect, trustworthy, and righteous altogether. We don’t trim it for PR; we receive it.
- After the reading I’d say, “Poetry can use pictures to tell true things. The same God who made the heavens also spoke in Scripture, and we listen carefully to what He actually wrote.”

At lunch with my 10-year-old, Bible open:
- I’d read Psalm 19 again and then read Genesis 1:1–5. I’d show the difference between poetry (parallel lines, rich imagery) and narrative prose (the repeated, time-anchored sequence: “And God said… and it was so… and there was evening and there was morning, the nth day”). Genesis 1 is elevated, patterned prose, but it reads as history, not Hebrew poetry.
- I’d connect Scripture with Scripture:
  - Exodus 20:11 ties the creation week to our week: “For in six days the LORD made heaven and earth…” That’s given as the reason for Sabbath rhythm.
  - Jesus treats Genesis as history: “From the beginning of creation, ‘God made them male and female’” (Mark 10:6).
  - Paul treats Adam as a real man in Romans 5 and 1 Corinthians 15.
- I’d say, “We love brothers and sisters who see details differently, but we never call something ‘poetry’ just to make people like us. We ask, ‘What did God actually say, and how did He say it?’”

About the elder’s comment:
- I would not correct him from the pew or sour my child’s spirit. I’d set up a gracious, private conversation that week. I’d ask:
  - “When you say ‘for unity,’ do you mean the text itself demands a poetic reading, or are we choosing it to avoid offense?”
  - “How do you see the genre markers of Psalm 19 versus Genesis 1?”
  - “How do you read Exodus 20:11, Mark 10:6, Romans 5?” 
  - “When Scripture speaks of a ‘stumbling block,’ it’s Christ crucified (1 Corinthians 1:23). Are we in danger of removing the wrong offense?”
- I’d make it clear I want unity in truth: we can show charity on age-of-earth discussions, but we must not re-label a passage to mute its claims. Inerrancy means all Scripture is true in the way each passage intends to speak.

What I want my 10-year-old to carry home:
- All Scripture is God’s Word and therefore trustworthy.
- Different genres speak truly in different ways; metaphors are real communication, not make-believe.
- We don’t adjust the Bible to fit the room; we adjust ourselves to fit the Bible.
- We honor our leaders, pray for them, and test everything by the Word like the Bereans (Acts 17:11).

Then we’d memorize a verse together—either Psalm 19:1 or Psalm 19:7—and thank God that His world and His Word never contradict, because He is the Author of both.

Ryan, I’d use Psalm 19 to disciple my child in how genre works without ever weakening truth.

- During (or right after) the reading I’d quietly say: “This is a poem. Poems use pictures—‘the sun in a tent,’ ‘the heavens declare’—but the things it says are 100% true: God made the world and His word is perfect.” Then I’d point to verses 1–6 (creation speaks) and 7–11 (God’s law speaks) and say, “Both are God’s truth.”

- I’d connect it to Genesis 1: “Some adults say Genesis 1 is ‘poetic’ or ‘artful.’ Even if God told the story in a beautiful, patterned way, it still tells what really happened: God created everything by His word, made people in His image, made them male and female, called creation very good, ordered it with purpose, and set a rhythm of work and rest.” I’d make clear that poetry never means “not true”; it means “true things said with pictures.”

- If my child asks about the “days,” I’d say, “Christians who love the Bible have differed on whether those days are 24-hour days or a literary frame. What we don’t differ on is that God actually created and that Adam and Eve were real and the fall was real. We hold to what Scripture clearly affirms.”

- With the elder, I’d thank him for seeking unity, then gently suggest a better path: “Let’s guard unity by affirming inerrancy and the core creation truths, while allowing some room on the length of the days. But let’s not call Genesis ‘poetry’ in a way that makes people think it’s less than history, just as we don’t treat Psalm 19’s claims about God’s law as ‘only poetry.’” I’d ask that we teach our people how genre works rather than using genre to downgrade meaning.

Practically, next Sunday I’d have my 10‑year‑old memorize Psalm 19:1 and 19:7 with me, then read Genesis 1 together that afternoon, pointing out the repeated phrases. I’d finish with, “God’s world is true. God’s word is true. And God tells His truth in many beautiful ways.”

*The pew wood gives a small groan as I lean in, the Psalm floating over us like a breeze through tall grass. My boy’s knee is swinging, heel ticking the hymnal rack, eyes trying to track the reader’s voice.*

I tap the bulletin where they’ve printed the text and whisper, just loud enough for him alone. "Listen for the part where it says the sky talks."

*The line comes — the sky proclaims — and I see his mouth shape the word before it lands in his ears. I don’t add a lecture. I let the room carry it. My hand stays on the page, steady, so he has something to anchor to while the words run their course like the sun it’s about to name.*

When the reader hits "a tent for the sun," I nudge him once and point, not up — that would be theater — but to the margin where I’ve drawn a small circle and an arrow from east to west. He grins like he’s been let in on a secret that’s not actually a secret.

After the Amen, I don’t pick a fight in the aisle. I shake the elder’s hand, same as always, and mean it. Unity is a good word if you don’t use it to sand the truth down. There’s a time for the table and a calm question; this moment belongs to my son.

So I take him outside. *The noon light is blunt and warm; the air smells like dust and cut grass.* I hold my palm flat at the eastern edge of the sky and sweep it across to the west. "What did the Psalm say the sun does?"

"Runs," he says. "Like a happy runner."

"That’s right." I keep my voice plain. "Poetry’s the way we talk when the real thing is too big to say straight. It doesn’t make it less true; it makes it land. That’s the sky. That’s the sun God set to run it. You can watch it if you’re patient."

We stand there for the kind of minute a ten-year-old can bear. A swallow cuts a dark stitch high over the roofline. I don’t push past the hold he has. I just give him one more line to carry. "Next time you hear someone say it’s ‘just poetry,’ try this in your head: it’s poetry, and it’s true. Both."

Later in the week, I ask the elder for a quiet talk. "Help me, brother," I say, not sharp. "My boy heard Psalm 19 on Sunday. I want him to know the words he hears in here still mean what they name out there. If we call the beginning ‘just’ poetry for the sake of peace, what are we teaching him when he looks up?" Then I listen. I can carry unity and truth at the same time if I keep my hands open and my feet on the ground.

But next Sunday? In the pew, with my ten-year-old? I point to the word sky when it’s read. I take him outside to stand under it. I let the day preach the rest.

*I slide the psalter a little closer so my boy can see, the morning sun laying a red stripe through the stained glass across our hands. The reader’s voice rolls steady from up front.* 

I bend and whisper, keeping it soft so it doesn’t carry. "Listen for it—'the sky is talking about God.' Hear that? 'Their voice goes out.'" *I tap the line with a finger, then point toward the bright square of window.* "That’s today. Right there."

When the line comes—"a tent for the sun"—I nudge him, not a joke, just a note. "Watch it run its path," I murmur. "From that side to that side. Like a runner. You see it every day, and the psalm just says it plain."

*The reading turns to the part about the Lord’s instruction, perfect, reviving the soul. I don’t explain it; I let it land. My hand rests on his shoulder; we stand when it’s time; we sit when it’s time. I want him to feel that the words are good before he’s taught to second-guess them.*

On the steps after, while folks trade greetings, I keep it easy. "What did you hear?" I ask him. Not a quiz. Let him pick one piece. If he says "the sky talks," I grin and squint up with him into the bright. "Then we’ll listen on the walk home."

If the elder comes by and the talk turns, I keep my voice steady. "I’ll always make room to bear with a brother," I say, "but I won’t teach my son to treat God’s words like a problem to solve. He heard Psalm 19, and then he looked up. That’s the kind of unity I want—us standing under the same sky, letting the text say what it says."

*We start down the path, heat gathering in the eastern sky, the bell’s last hum fading behind us. I make a small plan: when the sun touches the far edge tonight, I’ll point again, not to win an argument, just to keep him company while the sky does its work.*

*I rest my palm on the smooth back of the pew, my boy’s shoulder warm against my sleeve, the old wood smelling a little like dust and lemon oil. When the reader begins Psalm 19, I lean close enough that he can hear without anyone else needing to.* 

"Listen for how the sky talks," I whisper. "Count it."

*The words roll out — the heavens declare, the sky shows, day to day pours out speech — and when we get to, In them he has set a tent for the sun, I tap the page in his small psalter, once, so he doesn’t miss it. He grins like he’s on a treasure hunt and I let the rest of the Psalm do its work without more whispering. No eye-rolling, no sideways looks toward the elder. Just the Psalm speaking, which is plenty.*

*After the benediction, we don’t hurry. We step outside into the bright cold and I squint east, where the sun’s a little higher now than when we came in.*

"That’s it," I say, tipping my chin toward the blue. "The sky telling without words. What’d you count?"

"Four," he says, holding up fingers. "Sky talking, day talking, night talking, the sun running."

"Good ears." *I point to the church wall’s shadow on the steps.* "Let’s mark this. See where the edge is now? We’ll check it again after lunch. Watch how the sun runs its path like the Psalm says."

*At the table later, with soup bowls pushed back and the window warmed by the light, I put Psalm 19 and Genesis 1 side by side. I don’t make a speech; I ask him to read the first lines out loud, boy-voice steady, and then the day-two line about God making the sky.*

"Some people use the word ‘poetry’ like it means ‘not real,’" I tell him, keeping my tone plain. "But poetry is how you say a thing so true your chest knows it’s true. The sky really does tell, and the sun really does run a course. We can see it. We can set a stick and watch the hours of the sun. That’s what these words are for — to teach us to look."

*I let that sit. He presses his palm to the warm glass and smiles.*

"If you ever hear a grown-up make the Bible smaller so nobody trips over it," I add, softer, "you don’t need to argue. Just don’t learn that habit. Our part is to hear what’s written and live gentle with people while we don’t pretend we didn’t hear."

*And I keep the elder out of it in front of my boy. Unity doesn’t need me to win a point at my son’s expense. Midweek, I ask the elder for ten minutes in his office. I bring only the text. I read Genesis 1 and Psalm 19 aloud, then ask two questions and leave them on the table:*

"What do you think David means if not what any child can see when he looks up — the sky declaring, the sun running its track?"

"And does unity ask us to pretend not to see what God taught our eyes to notice?"

*No cornering, no heat. Just the words and the sky outside his window doing what they do, same as always.*

"Come afternoon we’ll go check our shadow," I tell my boy, stacking the bowls. "We’ll see if the sun kept running like it always does."

Ryan, when your ten-year-old hears "the sky pours out speech night after night," what’s the first thing they point to when you step outside?
